subject: ap-world-history
theme: Global Conflict, 1900–present: Industrial warfare, totalitarianism, and global depression destroy the optimistic liberal order and reshape the global political landscape.

core_concepts.title: World War I: Causes, Conduct, and Consequences
core_concepts.summary: WWI's causes (MAIN mnemonic): Militarism (arms race, especially Germany-Britain naval rivalry), Alliance system (Triple Alliance: Germany, Austria-Hungary, Italy vs. Triple Entente: France, Russia, Britain), Imperialism (colonial competition and rivalries), Nationalism (especially in the Balkans — assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand, June 1914, triggered the alliance system). The war introduced industrial-scale killing: trench warfare, poison gas, machine guns, artillery barrages created a killing machine. The Treaty of Versailles (1919) imposed the 'war guilt clause,' reparations, territorial losses, and military restrictions on Germany — widely blamed for creating conditions for Hitler's rise.

core_concepts.title: WWI's Global Dimensions
core_concepts.summary: WWI was genuinely global. Colonial subjects from India, Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Middle East fought for their imperial masters — approximately 1.5 million Indian soldiers served under Britain. The Armenian Genocide (1915–1923): Ottoman authorities systematically deported and killed 1–1.5 million Armenians, using WWI as cover for ethnic cleansing. The Balfour Declaration (1917) promised British support for a Jewish homeland in Palestine, setting the stage for the Arab-Israeli conflict. The 1918 influenza pandemic — spread by troop movements — killed 50–100 million people, more than the war itself. Wilson's Fourteen Points promised self-determination but were not applied to colonies.

core_concepts.title: The Interwar Period: Depression, Fascism, and Totalitarianism
core_concepts.summary: The Great Depression (1929–1939) began with the US stock market crash and spread globally through interconnected financial markets and trade contractions. Mass unemployment and economic despair destabilized democratic governments in Germany, Italy, and Spain. Fascism — an authoritarian nationalist ideology glorifying the state, violence, and racial or national hierarchy — emerged as a response: Mussolini in Italy (1922), Hitler in Germany (1933), Franco in Spain (1939). Nazi ideology combined extreme nationalism, antisemitism, Social Darwinism, and the desire for Lebensraum (living space) in Eastern Europe. The Soviet Union under Stalin pursued a different totalitarianism — communist ideology with gulag labor camps, collectivization famines, and purges killing millions.

core_concepts.title: World War II: Global War and Holocaust
core_concepts.summary: WWII (1939–1945) began with German invasions of Poland (September 1939) after appeasement policies failed (Munich, 1938). Japan had begun its own expansionist war in China (1937 — the Nanking Massacre, ~200,000 killed) and expanded into Southeast Asia. The Holocaust: Nazi Germany systematically murdered 6 million Jews and 5–6 million others (Roma, disabled people, political opponents, Soviet POWs) through a bureaucratic apparatus of concentration and extermination camps. The Pacific War: Japan attacked Pearl Harbor (December 7, 1941); the US dropped atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki (August 1945), killing 130,000–226,000 instantly. Total WWII deaths: approximately 70–85 million, the deadliest conflict in human history.

core_concepts.title: Total War and Civilian Experience
core_concepts.summary: Both world wars demonstrated the concept of 'total war' — mobilization of entire economies, societies, and populations for warfare. Home fronts: women entered industrial workforces in unprecedented numbers; governments controlled economies, rationed food, and directed propaganda. Civilian targeting became systematic: German strategic bombing of London (Blitz); Allied firebombing of Dresden and Tokyo; Japanese bombing of Chinese cities; nuclear attacks on Japan. The moral boundaries of warfare collapsed. Colonial subjects were mobilized for European wars with promises of post-war rights and autonomy that were largely unfulfilled, intensifying anti-colonial nationalism.

core_concepts.title: Post-War Order: UN, Nuremberg, and Human Rights
core_concepts.summary: WWII's devastation generated new international institutions and legal frameworks. The United Nations (founded 1945) aimed to prevent future wars through collective security and diplomacy. The Nuremberg Trials (1945–46) established that individuals could be held accountable under international law for 'crimes against humanity' and genocide — creating the precedent for modern international criminal justice. The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) proclaimed universal, inalienable rights for all people regardless of nationality or race. The Bretton Woods institutions (IMF, World Bank, GATT) established a liberal international economic order under US leadership.

key_terms.definition: Mnemonic for WWI's causes: Militarism, Alliance system, Imperialism, Nationalism — together creating the conditions for a local crisis to explode into world war.
WWI's dominant combat system — opposing armies dug elaborate trench networks facing each other across a 'no-man's land,' resulting in massive casualties for minimal territorial gains.
WWI peace settlement imposing the 'war guilt clause,' massive reparations, territorial losses, and military restrictions on Germany; widely regarded as a cause of WWII.
Systematic Ottoman deportation and mass killing of 1–1.5 million Armenians during WWI; the 20th century's first genocide, though Turkey still disputes the classification.

British statement promising 'favourable consideration' for a Jewish homeland in Palestine while pledging not to harm existing Palestinian Arab communities — contradictory promise setting stage for future conflict.
US President Wilson's 1918 peace proposal including national self-determination, freedom of the seas, and a League of Nations; applied to Europe but not to colonized peoples in Asia and Africa.
Authoritarian nationalist ideology glorifying the state, violence, and racial hierarchy; rejecting liberalism and communism; exemplified by Mussolini's Italy and Hitler's Nazi Germany.
Nazi ideology calling for German expansion into Eastern Europe to acquire 'living space' for the German Volk, displacing or eliminating Slavic peoples — a key motive for WWII's eastern campaigns.
Nazi Germany's systematic genocide of 6 million Jews and 5–6 million others (Roma, disabled, Soviet POWs, political opponents) through industrialized mass murder in concentration camps.
Modern warfare mobilizing entire economies, societies, and populations — blurring the distinction between soldiers and civilians and directing military action at enemy societies, not just armies.
British and French policy of making concessions to Hitler's territorial demands (culminating at Munich, 1938) to avoid war; widely judged to have encouraged further aggression.

International military tribunal trying Nazi leaders for war crimes and crimes against humanity; established the principle that individuals are accountable under international law regardless of state orders.
UN document proclaiming that all humans possess inherent rights regardless of race, nationality, or religion — foundational document of modern international human rights law.
US government program that developed the atomic bomb during WWII; bombs dropped on Hiroshima (August 6) and Nagasaki (August 9), 1945, killing 130,000–226,000 people and ending the Pacific War.
Japanese military killing of approximately 200,000 Chinese civilians and prisoners of war following the capture of Nanjing — exemplifies Japanese imperial violence in China.

exam_tip: Unit 7 essays often ask about causation (what caused WWI or WWII) or continuity and change (how did total war change from WWI to WWII). For causation, go beyond the assassination of Franz Ferdinand — it was a trigger, not a cause. The deeper causes were the alliance system and military planning that made local conflicts automatically escalate. For WWII, always connect interwar economic crisis (Great Depression) → political instability → rise of totalitarianism → WWII. The Holocaust is tested as an example of the extreme consequences of racism and totalitarianism — connect it to Unit 6's Social Darwinism ideology.
